channel.title: Comments for page "Di Molte Voci"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Di Molte Voci"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Italian here (and I live in Sicily too, not kidding)!
Well, there isn't a direct translation of "out of many voices", so the best alternative would be "Una di molte voci" which means "one out of many voices"; if you give me more details about what the name should mean, I can help you find a better one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it, the way it follows the "item# - class - ConProc - Description - Addenda/backstory" format is very organic, you can almost feel the snippets overtaking the document like barnacles on a boat's hull.

I examined Ms. Lanza's home. It had nothing of interest, and in fact very little at all. It's consistent with the theory that she was struggling with money.- Strunk

Is "money" not red here to show that the mask only reacts to "money in relation to it" to quote an earlier comment? It just looked like a mistake, IMO just a little hint that it was intentional could have improved it. I doubt anyone could piece it together without your comment, which is a shame because "The Voci is randomly triggered by that one particular lexical field" (default assumption if no hints are provided) is much less cool than "monetary exchanges under our current system are warped by authorities, which makes monetary exchanges related to the mask qualify as a target for its effects".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think we have a 2521.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what happens if you get a D-class to talk about Di Molte Voci … THEN SUDDENLY describe 2521? How in the world will 2521 get the information off of the foundation?

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
With 12 voiced characters, I called in ten other SCP YouTubers and made one heck of a reading for this one. I love the concept and the format, and I was really glad to be able to make a good recording of it.

Site-42 and 10 other SCP Readers Rendition of Di Molte Voci

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, it was just a thought :) I love this either way

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I need the readers to be able to understand them, and this is not an Italian-language wiki, so they will remain translated into English. The anomalous effect is already established to be more complex and difficult-to-characterize than is strictly presented in the text, so I don't have a problem with just saying "yeah it lets you translate stuff".

And then, naturally, it's customary to translate fluent text in one language to fluent text in another.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved this, +1!

Communism will win
One small note: I realize it's a wee bit nitpickyish, but if the mask's effect is that it turns all information about it into literal quotes from those who talked about it, then I think it would make more sense if the "Anonymous civilian" comments were not in such perfect English.
Maybe writing them in Italian would be too much (although I could help you with that if you wanted), but I feel like broken English would make them feel more authentic. Most people in Italy (and this is especially true in the south) don't know more then a couple dozen words in English, except youngsters, and it is unlikely that you would hear this kind of good English unless you were talking to university students (and even then, it would be the exception rather than the rule)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very original. I love it.

item.title: oh look it's +100
item.content:encoded: neat

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Come to change the page title, stay for the well executed format screw. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I swear I had this idea last night. Non-standard format, multiple researchers contributing, a jester theme item. This is spooky in its similarity actually. This is better than what I had in mind though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How come they were able to put it in the 2864 slot? Perhaps you should add something like "It seems that as long as we use an informal title, it can remain in a slot, as long as we don't use the designation, but now I can't figure out which slot it is in, can someone check the database files?". It would remove the problem of the fact that it still has a number on the website.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not greyscale, I mostly have issues with reds. Just wanted to make sure I didn't miss any hidden links in them =]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They are fairly similar, though- my color-blind-friendlyness simulator says unless you're true greyscale colorblind you're seeing about the same contrast/relative hue difference as anybody else. Link color being a few notches darker and bluer is the main distinction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That would be the colorblindness. The exact shade of red is pretty distinct from the standard link color.

Glad you enjoyed the article though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An SCP written entirely in epistolary form?
Up The Down Staircase is one of my most favorite books ever, and therefore I can't not upvote this for the format alone.

N.B. Was it intentional that the money colour is confusable enough for link colour or is it my colourblindness acting up? Please advise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Works for me. Thinking of language as the product of collective contributions combined with the informality of the name lends it credence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They sort of informally agreed to it, and apart from the Italian language itself, it's not referring to any source of authority for a meaning, like "SCP-2864" might. If it helps to think of the name as a 'borderline' case, then do that — SCP designations are affected, but 'Di Molte Voci' is not, and that helps delineate the effects of the anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I may not understand this very well, but if the scip's official designation cannot be expressed, why is naming it Di Molte Voci any different? Isn't it still an authoritative, final designation of sorts and, thereby, subject to the object's anomalous effects?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Usually, format-screw doesn't work; Sometimes, format-screw works; and on occasion, format-screw works VERY well. This is one of those occasions.

You seem to have done a lovely job of capturing the style of the inter-office memos of the Foundation and I love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The latter. Fixed the title. Thanks for pointing it out — I would have never noticed, since it's all Greek Italian to me anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should this be Di Molte Volci or Di Molte Voci?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If there's one thing that I'm always a total slut for on this wiki, it's a clever format screw. I am the easier sell in the world for those. So while my upvote on this one may be a given, I just have to comment that this is one of the most cleverly-presented meta articles I've seen in ages. So good work there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That actually makes sense. I don't think I've seen "has multiple, verifiable but untested effects" before. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Medical doctors would've done more tests.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aye, she's my go-to whenever I need a memeticist character that can hold a normal conversation.

I almost went with 'swarm memeplexes', but I thought nah, that's not nearly cool enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
lingchi memeplexes

Snerk.

Nice to see Vanessa Graff pop up again. One of the more underappreciated characters…

…ey? ey? eyyyyy?…

…I'll show myself out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, it has to do with consensus and authority. Anything banking hard enough on either is susceptible — or at least, that's the simplified version. Money (and commerce more generally) is kind of both — its value is purely a matter of consensus (i.e. enough people and merchants accepting it in exchange for goods), but of the sort that's infused with authority — it's backed up by governments, corporations, and militaries (you wouldn't get into the business otherwise) and more often than not, you play by its rules.

There's something about money that sets the mask off in this specific way, or at least, money in relation to it. This might be instead of, or in addition to, some other effect that happens when you sell it. The Foundation hasn't tested this, since there's no reason to test it, and it seems that Greta Lanza might have thought it was a bad idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn. I've always wanted to do an article where the effects prevented changing the text and the whole thing was made up of addenda. I think this does a similar idea much better.

Also, it's such a complete format screw that it took me a while (until I checked the URL) to realize it wasn't a tale! I also feel like I'm missing something. It obviously makes it so that you can't summarize or change information written about it, but what about the red-highlighted commerce-related words, or the fact that the old woman insisted on bartering for it? This is really intriguing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The non-expository exposition is beautifully done.
"We dance in a circle and suppose
But the Secret sits in the middle and knows."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Format screws really only work if they're justified in universe and fit the theme. Fortunately, this does both very, very well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps they assumed all these doctors were medical doctors?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah, I'm a sociology student. I'm not sure why medical experience would be relevant here, though. Glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it. Do you have medical experience of your own?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure what to make of it but damn if the format and progression itself isn't compelling.

item.title: new scp please read it bottom text
item.content:encoded:
I asked myself "What if there was an entire SCP made out of doctors' notes?", and went from there. Thanks to everyone who gave feedback!

And yes, this is an SCP, not a tale. Pls do not fix. Thanx.
